paragraphs: Bulida (Spanish Bulida or Bulida) is a traditional Spanish apricot associated with the south-east of Spain, the regions of Murcia, Alicante and Valencia, and dating to around 1940. It is prized in Spain as a hardy, heavy-cropping commercial variety that is frost and drought resistant and adapts to a wide range of soils.

The fruit is large with firm, yellow-orange skin (typically without a red blush) and yellow-orange flesh that is very sweet, juicy and melting. It is a freestone, with the flesh lifting cleanly from the stone. These qualities, plus its firmness and good keeping, make Bulida a favourite for fresh eating, canning, drying and the preserving and confectionery industries.

claims.text: Traditional Spanish apricot from around 1940, associated with south-east Spain (Murcia, Alicante, Valencia)
Hardy, heavy-cropping variety that is frost and drought resistant and adapts to many soils
Large fruit with firm yellow-orange skin (no red blush) and yellow-orange flesh
Very sweet, juicy, melting flesh that is freestone
Used for fresh eating, canning, drying and the preserving and confectionery industries

sources.name: Rare Fruit Club WA: Apricot Varieties
CSIC: Growth and phenological stages of Bulida apricot trees in south-east Spain

paragraphs: Castlebrite is an early season Californian apricot that originated in Fresno through the work of J.H. Weinberger of the USDA Agricultural Research Service. It came from open pollinated seed of the selection B60-12, itself a cross of Perfection and Castleton, and was developed and recommended as an early shipping variety for San Joaquin Valley conditions.

The fruit is medium sized, firm, and bright orange with a slight red blush. Flavour is semi sweet but can be quite tart unless the fruit is fully ripe, so it is best left to sweeten on the tree. Castlebrite is self fruitful, meaning it does not need a separate pollinator, and it ripens roughly four weeks ahead of Blenheim, making it one of the earliest apricots of the season.

claims.text: Originated in Fresno, California, by J.H. Weinberger of the USDA Agricultural Research Service
Came from an open pollinated seedling of B60-12, a cross of Perfection and Castleton
Recommended as an early shipping variety productive under San Joaquin Valley conditions
Medium sized, firm fruit, bright orange with a slight red blush
Semi sweet flavour that is very tart unless fully ripe
Self fruitful, ripening about four weeks before Blenheim, one of the earliest apricots

sources.name: UC Davis Foundation Plant Services: Castlebrite apricot

paragraphs: Cot N Candy is an interspecific apricot, marketed as a white aprium, bred by Gary, Grant, and Leith Zaiger of Zaiger Genetics and protected under US Plant Patent 17827. Rather than being a pure apricot, its parentage weaves together apricot, plum, and plumcot, described in the patent as apricot crossed with ((plum by plumcot) by plumcot) by apricot. That mixed lineage is the whole point of the variety.

Unusually for an apricot, the flesh is white rather than orange, set behind velvety orange yellow skin with a reddish pink blush. The patent describes a regular, productive tree bearing medium sized, white fleshed fruit of excellent eating quality, and growers note a sweet, juicy flavour with melon and plum notes alongside the apricot. It is self fruitful and ripens in the early to middle part of summer.

claims.text: An interspecific apricot, sold as a white aprium, bred by Gary, Grant, and Leith Zaiger of Zaiger Genetics
Protected under US Plant Patent 17827
Parentage combines apricot, plum, and plumcot: apricot by ((plum by plumcot) by plumcot) by apricot
Has white flesh rather than the usual orange, with skin carrying a reddish pink blush
Sweet, juicy fruit of excellent eating quality
Self fruitful and ripens early to mid summer

sources.name: US Plant Patent 17827: Interspecific tree named Cot-N-Candy

paragraphs: Divinity is a South Australian apricot that has been grown in Australia since the 1950s. It is valued as an early-season variety, ripening from late November into early December, well ahead of the classic Moorpark. The fruit is firm and slightly oval (oblong) with a light to deep orange flesh and an attractive red blush on the skin.

The flavour is sweet and juicy with a slightly acid skin, making it good for eating fresh as well as for jam, stewing, drying and preserving. A useful trait for the home grower is that Divinity is self-fertile, so it sets fruit without needing a second apricot nearby for pollination.

claims.text: South Australian variety developed in the 1950s and long established in Australia since that decade
Early-season variety ripening from late November to early December (before Moorpark)
Fruit is firm and slightly oval (oblong) with light to deep orange flesh and a red blush
Sweet, juicy flavour with a slightly acid skin; used fresh and for jam, stewing, drying and preserving
Self-fertile, so no separate pollinator is required

sources.name: Rare Fruit Club WA: Apricot Varieties
Lawn.com.au: Apricot Tree Varieties in Australia

paragraphs: Fireball is one of the earliest apricots grown in Australia, with fruit ready from late November into December. It is a low-chill variety, which lets it crop in warm temperate and Mediterranean climates where many traditional apricots will not, and it is widely sold in a compact dwarf form for small gardens.

The fruit has vibrant orange to orange-red skin and sweet, juicy flesh that lifts cleanly from the stone, making it a freestone apricot. Fireball is only partially self-fertile, so while it can set some fruit alone, a second apricot nearby or hand pollination improves the crop.

claims.text: One of the earliest-ripening apricots in Australia, ready from late November into December
Low-chill variety suited to warm temperate and Mediterranean climates
Vibrant orange to orange-red skin with sweet, juicy flesh
Flesh separates cleanly from the stone (freestone)
Only partially self-fertile; cross-pollination improves yield

sources.name: Rare Fruit Club WA: Apricot Varieties
Lawn.com.au: Apricot Tree Varieties in Australia

paragraphs: Glengarry stands out as a low-chill apricot, one of the few that will crop in subtropical and warm-temperate parts of Australia where most apricots fail to get enough winter cold. It is among the earliest to ripen, fruiting through spring into early summer, and it is self-fertile, so a single tree will set a crop.

The tree is large, vigorous and a heavy bearer. Its fruit is small to medium and pale golden with a slight blush, with light apricot-coloured flesh, good flavour and a freestone. That makes Glengarry well suited to fresh eating as well as jams and preserves, especially for growers in milder climates.

claims.text: Low-chill variety, one of the few apricots that will crop in subtropical and warm-temperate regions
One of the earliest apricots to ripen, fruiting through spring into early summer
Self-fertile, setting a crop without a separate pollinator
Large, vigorous, heavy-bearing tree
Small to medium pale golden fruit with a slight blush, light apricot flesh and a freestone
Good for fresh eating, jams and preserves

sources.name: Rare Fruit Club WA: Apricot Varieties

paragraphs: Goldrich is an apricot introduced by Washington State University, bred in the cooler apricot country of the Pacific Northwest and long used as a benchmark for large, firm, good flavoured fruit. Virginia Cooperative Extension trials recorded it as a mid season bloomer that ripens early, carrying large fruit of around two and a half ounces.

The fruit is distinctive for its flattened, oblong shape and smooth suture, with an orange red blush over an orange yellow background. The flesh is orange, moderately firm, juicy, aromatic, and mildly subacid, and the fruit was rated attractive and good for fresh use. Goldrich is not self fertile: it requires a pollinator, and notably it cannot be pollinated by Perfection, so a compatible second variety is needed for a crop.

claims.text: Introduced by Washington State University
Blooms in mid season and ripens early, bearing large fruit of about 2.3 to 2.5 ounces
Fruit is flattened and oblong with a smooth suture and an orange red blush over an orange yellow background
Flesh is orange, moderately firm, juicy, aromatic, and mildly subacid, rated good for fresh use
Not self fertile: requires a pollinator and cannot be pollinated by Perfection

sources.name: Virginia Cooperative Extension (Virginia Tech): 1988-1995 Apricot Variety Evaluations in Virginia, Publication 422-761

paragraphs: Hunter is an Australian apricot, reported to have originated in South Australia around 1966. Its main claim to distinction is timing: it is one of the latest apricots to ripen, cropping from mid to late January and so extending the season after most other varieties have finished.

The fruit is large, firm, and plump, with deep orange flesh that is sweet, juicy, and full flavoured. It is well regarded both for fresh eating and especially for home drying, and it is self pollinating, so it does not need another variety nearby to set a crop.

claims.text: Australian apricot reported to have originated in South Australia around 1966
One of the latest ripening apricots, cropping mid to late January and extending the season
Large, firm, plump fruit with deep orange, sweet, juicy flesh
Valued for fresh eating and especially for home drying
Self pollinating, needing no separate pollinator

sources.name: Rare Fruit Club WA: Apricot Varieties (Hunter)

paragraphs: Katy is a low chill apricot bred by Zaiger Genetics and prized as a favourite for warm winter climates where many apricots fail to set. It needs only around 200 to 300 chill hours, and is self fruitful, so a single tree will crop without a separate pollinator. It ripens early, roughly three to four weeks ahead of Blenheim (Royal).

The fruit is large, firm, and freestone, so the flesh parts cleanly from the stone. Tree ripened Katy is subacid rather than tart, with a sweet, juicy, low acid flavour, and it is an all purpose apricot used fresh as well as for cooking, canning, drying, and preserves.

claims.text: Bred by Zaiger Genetics and favoured for warm winter, low chill climates
Requires only about 200 to 300 chill hours
Self fruitful, needing no separate pollinator
Large, firm, freestone fruit
Tree ripe fruit is subacid rather than tart, sweet and juicy
Ripens early, about three to four weeks before Blenheim (Royal)

sources.name: Ask The Green Genie: Low Chill Apricots (Katy)

paragraphs: Moorpark is an old English apricot, taking its name from the Moor Park estate in Hertfordshire and first sold under that name in the late 1700s. It has long been regarded as a benchmark for apricot flavour.

The fruit is large with deep orange skin, a heady aroma and rich, sweet flavour, and the flesh is freestone when fully ripe. It is a late-ripening variety. Moorpark is self-fertile, so a single tree will set fruit.

claims.text: Old English apricot named after the Moor Park estate in Hertfordshire, first sold under that name in the late 1700s
Large fruit, deep orange skin, heady aroma and rich sweet flavour
Freestone flesh when fully ripe
Late-ripening variety
Self-fertile

sources.name: Grow Great Fruit: Apricot tree varieties

paragraphs: Patterson is a California apricot from the breeding program of Fred Anderson at Le Grand, often called the father of the modern nectarine. It began as an F2 open pollinated seedling of Perfection, selected for testing in 1959 and released in 1968 to Burchell Nursery. Anderson and Irvin Burchell set out to develop a canning apricot that was a regular, reliable bearer and would resist pit burning, and it was named for Patterson, California, a town long associated with the crop.

Bred first for canning, Patterson is also well suited to drying, concentrate, and the fresh market, which made it a workhorse commercial variety. It produces medium to large, firm, uniform orange fruit that ships well, so it is one of the apricots most often found in supermarkets. Harvest falls in the mid to late part of the apricot season.

claims.text: Bred by Fred Anderson at Le Grand, California, as an F2 open pollinated seedling of Perfection, selected for testing in 1959
Released in 1968 to Burchell Nursery, a California wholesale nursery
Bred chiefly as a canning apricot that was a regular bearer and resisted pit burning
All purpose fruit suited to canning, drying, concentrate, and the fresh market
Medium to large, firm, uniform orange fruit that ships well and is common in supermarkets
Named after the town of Patterson, California, long associated with apricot growing

sources.name: Good Fruit Grower: Patterson, an all-round apricot

paragraphs: Rival is an apricot often described as similar to Goldrich and is best known for producing a large tree carrying heavy crops of large, superior fruit. The fruit is large and oval, with light orange skin flushed or speckled red and deep orange flesh that is aromatic and slightly tart. It is typically a midseason to late variety.

A key point for growers is that Rival is not reliably self-fertile and needs cross-pollination from an early-blooming apricot to set a good crop. Sources differ on its exact origin (some describe it as Canadian), so the country of breeding is treated as uncertain here, but it is documented as a parent of later Washington-bred apricots such as Tomcot.

claims.text: Similar to Goldrich and produces a large tree with heavy crops of large, superior fruit
Large, oval fruit with light orange skin flushed or speckled red and deep orange flesh
Aromatic, slightly tart flavour
Needs cross-pollination from an early-blooming pollinator (not reliably self-fertile)
Used as a parent of the Washington-bred Tomcot apricot

sources.name: Rare Fruit Club WA: Apricot Varieties

paragraphs: Storeys (also written Storey, and known as Early Moorpark or Moorpark Early) is an Australian apricot best understood as a faster-growing, earlier-fruiting version of the much-loved Moorpark. It ripens roughly three weeks before Moorpark, from about mid-December into January, which extends the apricot season at the early end.

The fruit is large with deep golden colouring and some red blush, and firm, deep-yellow flesh with good flavour. It is self-fertile, so it does not need a pollination partner, and it is a versatile all-rounder used for eating fresh, jam, bottling, canning, preserving and drying.

claims.text: Australian apricot, a faster-growing, earlier-fruiting version of Moorpark, also called Early Moorpark / Moorpark Early
Ripens about three weeks before Moorpark, roughly mid-December into January
Large fruit with deep golden colour and some red blush, and firm deep-yellow flesh
Self-fertile, needing no separate pollinator
Used for eating fresh, jam, bottling, canning, preserving and drying

sources.name: Rare Fruit Club WA: Apricot Varieties
Lawn.com.au: Apricot Tree Varieties in Australia

paragraphs: Tardif de Bordaneil is a French apricot from a chance seedling found in the Eastern Pyrenees Orientales around 1970. Tardif means late, and that is its defining trait: it is among the latest known apricots, both in flowering and in ripening. The very late bloom helps it escape spring frosts, while harvest comes well into late summer, extending the apricot season at its tail end.

The fruit is large to very large and slightly oblong, with light orange skin overlaid by an intense red flush. The flesh is firm, juicy, sweet, and fragrant, making it a good quality eating apricot. It is self fertile, vigorous, and productive, so a single tree will crop on its own.

claims.text: French apricot from a seedling found in the Eastern Pyrenees Orientales around 1970
One of the latest known apricots in both flowering and ripening, helping it escape spring frosts
Large to very large, slightly oblong fruit with light orange skin and an intense red flush
Firm, juicy, sweet, and fragrant flesh
Self fertile, vigorous, and productive

sources.name: Rare Fruit Club WA: Apricot Varieties (Tardif de Bordaneil)
Heritage Fruit Trees: Tardif de Bordaneil apricot

paragraphs: Tilton is a classic American apricot that began as a chance seedling discovered by J.E. Tilton in California in 1885, at a time when the state's dried-apricot trade was expanding. It quickly became a commercial standard, partly because the tree proved hardy and relatively tolerant of stormy weather and late frosts. It is also cold hardy and self-fertile.

The fruit is medium to large and heart-shaped, with glowing golden-yellow skin, a sunny blush and a distinct suture. The flesh is firm, fine-textured and aromatic, with a good balance of sweetness and acidity, and it is freestone at maturity. Tilton holds its colour and shape when preserved, which is why it is rated one of the best varieties for canning and drying as well as for fresh eating.

claims.text: Chance seedling found by J.E. Tilton in California in 1885
Hardy tree, tolerant of stormy weather and late frosts, and cold hardy
Fruit is medium to large, heart-shaped, golden-yellow with a blush and a distinct suture
Firm, fine-textured, aromatic flesh with a balance of sweetness and acidity; freestone
Rated one of the best varieties for canning and drying as well as fresh eating
Self-fertile

sources.name: Rare Fruit Club WA: Apricot Varieties
Minneopa Orchards: The Tilton Apricot Tree

paragraphs: Trevatt is an Australian heirloom apricot dating to the early 1900s and long grown as a commercial variety. It is one of the classic old Australian apricots.

The fruit is large and rounded with light yellow skin, deeper orange flesh and a highly aromatic, flavourful taste. It ripens early to mid season, before Moorpark, and its firm flesh makes it popular for drying and preserving as well as fresh eating. Trevatt is self-fertile, though a second tree can improve cropping.

claims.text: Australian heirloom apricot from the early 1900s, grown commercially
Large rounded fruit, light yellow skin with deeper orange flesh, highly aromatic and flavourful
Ripens early to mid season, before Moorpark
Firm flesh suited to drying and preserving
Self-fertile, with cropping improved by a second tree
